questions.question: In the context of graph theory, what is the difference between a trail and a path? Provide a small graph example where a trail exists that is not a path.
expectedPoints: Definition of a trail: a walk with no repeated edges.
Definition of a path: a walk with no repeated vertices.
Explanation that a path is always a trail, but a trail is not always a path.
Example: A graph with vertices A,B,C and edges AB, BC, CA. The walk A-B-C-A is a trail (no edge repeated) but not a path (vertex A is repeated).

questions.question: Explain the concept of graph connectivity. How do the definitions of walks, trails, and paths contribute to this concept? Prove that a graph is connected if and only if there exists a walk between every pair of vertices.
expectedPoints: Definition of a connected graph: there exists a path between every pair of distinct vertices.
Explanation that if a path exists, so does a walk and a trail (since a path is a special case of both).
Proof of 'if' direction: If a walk exists between every pair, then a path must exist (can shorten any walk to a path by removing cycles).
Proof of 'only if' direction: If the graph is connected (path exists between every pair), then a walk trivially exists by definition of a path.
Discussion on why the definition of connectivity relies on paths and not just walks (to avoid trivial, infinite walks).
